name: Havana Syndrome Full
description: Havana Syndrome refers to a series of unexplained health incidents reported by US embassy staff and other government employees stationed in Cuba, China, and other countries, beginning in late 2016. Affected individuals reported a range of symptoms, including sudden onset of intense head pressure, localized sound or vibration, dizziness, nausea, fatigue, cognitive difficulties (memory loss, difficulty concentrating), and visual disturbances. Some individuals have experienced persistent symptoms and long-term health problems. Proponents of the theory that Havana Syndrome is caused by a deliberate attack often suggest the use of directed energy weapons (such as microwaves or ultrasound) by a hostile foreign power (often Russia or China) as the most likely cause. They point to the geographically dispersed nature of the incidents, the specificity of symptoms reported, and the lack of other plausible explanations that account for all reported cases. Some proponents also highlight the historical use of directed energy weapons by various countries and the potential for such weapons to cause neurological damage. Mainstream scientific and governmental investigations, including those conducted by the CIA and the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, have acknowledged the genuineness of the reported symptoms and the impact on affected individuals. However, these investigations have been unable to definitively determine the cause of the incidents. While directed energy weapons are considered a possible cause, other potential explanations, such as mass psychogenic illness (mass hysteria), environmental factors, pre-existing medical conditions, or even the effects of surveillance equipment, have not been ruled out. The CIA's interim assessment in 2022 concluded that it was unlikely that a foreign adversary was responsible for the majority of reported cases, although it could not rule out foreign involvement in a subset of cases. Ongoing research aims to further investigate the potential causes and mechanisms underlying Havana Syndrome.
subtitle: Unexplained health incidents affecting US personnel.

claims.claim: Havana Syndrome is caused by directed energy weapons.
claims.evidence: Reports of localized sound/vibration, specific neurological symptoms, and lack of other definitive explanations.
claims.criticism: Mainstream investigations have not definitively proven directed energy weapons as the cause; other explanations like mass psychogenic illness or environmental factors are also considered.

claims.claim: A hostile foreign power is responsible for the attacks.
claims.evidence: Geopolitical tensions, potential motives for targeting US personnel, and historical use of directed energy weapons by some countries.
claims.criticism: The CIA's interim assessment found it unlikely that a foreign adversary was responsible for the majority of cases, although it could not rule out involvement in some instances.

claims.claim: The symptoms are not psychosomatic or due to mass psychogenic illness.
claims.evidence: Objective medical findings in some cases, the specificity of symptoms reported, and the persistence of symptoms over time.
claims.criticism: While some cases may have objective findings, the possibility of mass psychogenic illness contributing to some reports cannot be completely excluded, especially given the high-stress environment of diplomatic postings.

keyFigures.name: Marc Polymeropoulos
keyFigures.role: Former CIA officer who experienced Havana Syndrome symptoms.

keyFigures.name: James Giordano
keyFigures.role: Professor of neurology and biochemistry studying potential neurological effects.

keyFigures.name: CIA Investigation Team
keyFigures.role: Responsible for investigating the cause and scope of Havana Syndrome.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to curses or hexes causing illness, though highly speculative.

timeline.event: First reports of unexplained health incidents in Havana, Cuba.
Reports spread to other countries, including China.
National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine report acknowledges the genuineness of symptoms.
CIA releases interim assessment finding it unlikely that a foreign adversary was responsible for most cases.

sources.author: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
